worth_it_explanation: Content classification is the signal Index Exchange passes to buyers describing what a page or app is about, so it drives which campaigns target the inventory and which brand-safety filters clear or block a bid. A category that is missing, stale, or mislabeled either suppresses demand that would have bid or invites a brand-safety incident when an advertiser lands next to content it meant to avoid. The check is a review of the classification signals a publisher already sends through their site setup and header-bidding wrapper, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete labeling decision the publisher can correct.
intro: Content classification is how a publisher tells buyers what their inventory is about, using IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy categories carried in the bid request through fields such as the site or app category, section category, and page category. Index Exchange forwards those signals to demand partners, who use them for contextual targeting and brand-safety decisions, so the accuracy of the labels shapes both how much demand competes for the impression and how safely it clears. This subcategory looks at whether the classification a publisher sends is accurate, current, and specific enough for buyers to act on, rather than absent, mismatched, or stuck on a deprecated taxonomy version.

audit_checks.title: Content categories are missing or do not match the actual content
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the category signal is absent or wrong, buyers cannot target the inventory contextually and their brand-safety filters cannot judge it accurately. Missing categories suppress demand that would have bid on an accurate label, and mismatched categories can clear a bid the advertiser's filter would have blocked had the content been labeled correctly, so the price and the safety of the impression both turn on the label being right.

educate.base:
Content classification is the part of a bid request that tells a buyer what the page or app is about. In the OpenRTB protocol that Index Exchange (IX) speaks, the publisher's content is described with Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab Content Taxonomy categories carried in fields such as the site or app category, the section category, and the page category. A buyer reads those categories to decide whether the inventory fits a contextual campaign and whether it clears the advertiser's brand-safety rules. When the categories are accurate, both of those decisions can be made on real information about the content.

Two failure modes break that signal. The first is a category that is missing, where the bid request carries no content category at all, so a buyer running a contextual or brand-suitability strategy has nothing to match against and the impression falls out of consideration. The second is a category that is present but wrong, where a sports section is labeled as general news, or a single site-level category is stamped on every page regardless of what each one actually covers. A wrong label is worse than a missing one in the brand-safety direction since an advertiser's filter clears the impression on the strength of a label that does not describe the content.

The classification usually originates in the publisher's own configuration, set on the ad units or pages in the site setup and passed into the header-bidding wrapper as first-party data, then forwarded by Index Exchange to demand partners. That means the accuracy is the publisher's to control. A page about a sensitive news event and a page about a recipe should not share one blanket category, and a section that has changed focus since the categories were first assigned should not keep carrying the old label.

A few signs point to categories that are missing or inaccurate:
  • The bid request carries no content category, or carries only a single site-level category applied uniformly to every page
  • The category assigned to a section does not match what the section actually publishes, for example a finance vertical labeled as general lifestyle
  • Pages on clearly different topics, such as hard news and entertainment, all resolve to the same category with no page-level or section-level breakout
You may want to confirm that the content categories sent in the bid request reflect what each page and section actually covers. One approach is to inspect the categories on a sample of pages across the site's main sections and compare each label against the real content.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, sections often cover sharply different topics, from hard news to sports to lifestyle, and a single site-level category cannot describe all of them. You may want to assign categories at the section or page level so a finance article and a celebrity feature carry labels that match their content, which lets contextual demand find the right inventory and lets brand-safety filters judge each page on what it actually says.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages cover specific verticals such as electronics or apparel, while editorial and buying-guide pages cover broader topics. You may want to classify the commercial pages by their product vertical and the editorial pages by their subject, rather than stamping one store-wide category on everything, so buyers targeting a vertical can reach the pages that match it.

learn_more.source_title: First Party Data for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review the content categories carried in the bid request across a sample of pages and sections, and compare each label against the content it describes.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Open a representative page on the site and open the browser's developer tools to the network tab.
Filter the network requests for the header-bidding or Index Exchange bid request and inspect the OpenRTB payload.
Locate the content category fields, for example the site or app category, section category, and page category, and record the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy values present.
Repeat across pages from each main section and note whether the categories differ by section and page or whether one category applies uniformly.
Compare each recorded category against the actual content of the page and flag any that are missing or do not match.

site_config_review.label: Site and wrapper configuration review
steps: Review where content categories are set in the site setup and the header-bidding wrapper's first-party data configuration.
Record whether categories are assigned at the page or section level or only once at the site level.
Note any section whose assigned category no longer matches its current editorial focus.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per sampled page and section, the content categories carried in the bid request and whether each matches the actual content. A setup where categories are present, specific enough to distinguish sections, and accurate against the content passes. A setup where categories are absent, applied as a single blanket value across distinct content, or mismatched against what a page covers is a fail recorded with the pages and sections affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the content categories found in the bid request and whether each matches the page content above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} sends content categories in the bid request that are missing or do not match the actual content on a sample of pages and sections. Buyers read those Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy categories to target inventory contextually and to apply brand-safety filters, so a missing category suppresses demand that would have bid and a mismatched category can clear a bid the advertiser's filter should have blocked. {{voice_recommend}} assigning categories at the section or page level so each label reflects what the content actually covers, then confirming the corrected categories appear in the bid request. After the change, recheck a sample of pages to confirm the labels match the content and that distinct sections carry distinct categories.

audit_checks.title: Sensitive content is mislabeled rather than flagged for buyers
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Sensitive content that is labeled as benign clears brand-safety filters that would otherwise block it, which places an advertiser next to content it deliberately chose to avoid and exposes the publisher to demand pulling back once the mismatch surfaces. The harm reaches a brand-safety incident with real advertiser and revenue consequences rather than a missed targeting opportunity, which is why it sits above the routine labeling check.

educate.base:
Some content carries brand-safety weight that ordinary classification does not capture. Coverage of a tragedy, content touching on conflict, adult themes, or other sensitive subjects is exactly the content many advertisers set their filters to avoid. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab Content Taxonomy includes categories for sensitive topics, and the bid request can carry them so a buyer's brand-suitability rules can see the content for what it is and decide whether their campaign should run against it.

The failure this check looks for is sensitive content that is labeled as something benign, or labeled with a broad generic category that hides its nature, rather than flagged with the category that describes it. When a page covering a sensitive news event is sent to buyers as plain news or general content, an advertiser whose filter blocks sensitive topics never sees a reason to block, so the bid clears and the ad lands against content the advertiser meant to steer clear of. The label, not the content, is what the filter judged, and the label was wrong.

This matters more than a routine miscategorization because the cost is a brand-safety incident. An advertiser that discovers its ad ran beside content it had explicitly filtered out can lose trust in the supply path, and demand that pulls back hits the publisher's revenue on the inventory that needed the demand most. Flagging sensitive content honestly does the opposite. Buyers who are comfortable with the content still bid, buyers who are not opt out cleanly, and the publisher avoids serving an ad into a placement that becomes a complaint.

A few signs point to sensitive content being mislabeled rather than flagged:
  • Pages covering tragedy, conflict, or other sensitive subjects carry the same generic category as routine content with no sensitive-topic signal
  • The site has no process for tagging breaking or sensitive coverage differently from standard articles before it enters the auction
  • Brand-safety complaints or advertiser opt-outs have surfaced on content that the classification described as benign
You may want to confirm that sensitive content is flagged with a category that describes it rather than absorbed into a benign label. One approach is to review how breaking-news and sensitive coverage is classified compared with routine content, and check that the bid request carries a signal a buyer's brand-suitability filter can act on.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, breaking news and investigative coverage regularly touch sensitive subjects, and that content can attract high traffic at exactly the moment its classification matters most. You may want a process that tags sensitive coverage with an accurate category as it publishes, so a buyer's brand-suitability filter can opt out cleanly rather than discovering the placement after the ad has already served.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, sensitive material is rarer but can appear in editorial coverage, user reviews, or community pages that sit alongside the store. You may want to confirm that any such content is classified accurately rather than carrying the store's general category, so an advertiser's brand-safety rules judge it on its real subject.

learn_more.source_title: First Party Data for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review how sensitive content is classified compared with routine content, and confirm the bid request carries a category a buyer's brand-suitability filter can act on.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Identify a sample of pages covering sensitive subjects, for example breaking news on a tragedy or conflict, alongside a sample of routine pages.
Open each in the browser and inspect the header-bidding or Index Exchange bid request in the network tab.
Record the content categories carried for the sensitive pages and compare them with the categories on the routine pages.
Note whether the sensitive pages carry a category that describes their nature or whether they share the same generic label as routine content.

editorial_process_review.label: Editorial and classification process review
steps: Review whether the publisher has a process for tagging sensitive or breaking coverage differently from standard articles before it enters the auction.
Ask whether any brand-safety complaints or advertiser opt-outs have surfaced on content the classification described as benign, and record what was found.
Note whether sensitive-topic categories from the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy are available in the site and wrapper configuration and whether they are being used.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a sample of sensitive and routine pages, the content categories carried in the bid request and whether the sensitive pages are flagged with a category that describes them. A setup where sensitive content carries an accurate sensitive-topic category that buyers can filter on passes. A setup where sensitive content shares a benign or generic label with routine content, with no process to flag it before auction, is a fail recorded with the pages affected and any complaints or opt-outs tied to them.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record how sensitive content is classified compared with routine content above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} labels sensitive content with a benign or generic category rather than flagging it with a category that describes it. Buyers rely on the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy category in the bid request to apply brand-suitability filters, so sensitive coverage sent as ordinary content clears filters that would otherwise block it and places an advertiser next to content it chose to avoid. {{voice_recommend}} tagging sensitive and breaking coverage with an accurate category as it publishes, so a buyer's filter can opt out cleanly while comfortable demand still bids. After the change, recheck a sample of sensitive pages to confirm the bid request carries a signal that reflects the real subject.
